graph equivalences.

frontmatter.title: Path object structure on sections

mainmatter.p: A suitable path object on sections to a fixed function r \colon A \to B is given by a contravariant sum of path objects \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( s: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( s: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( s: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( s: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } } r \circ s \sim 1_{ B }, as sectionhood witnesses form a right-cloven family over their underlying sections (). In particular, an isomorphism of sections of r from \mathopen {} \left ( s,h \right ) \mathclose {} to \mathopen {} \left ( s',h' \right ) \mathclose {} is given by a homotopy H_s \colon s \sim s' together with a higher homotopy H_h \colon h \sim \mathopen {} \left ( \lambda x \mathpunct {.} \mathsf {act}_{ r } \mathopen {} \left ( H_s x \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h'x \right ) \mathclose {}.

frontmatter.title: Path object structure on retractions

mainmatter.p: A suitable path object on retractions to a fixed function s \colon B \to A is given by a contravariant sum of path objects \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( r: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( r: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( r: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( r: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } B \right ) \mathclose {} } } r \circ s \sim 1_{ B }, as retractionhood witnesses form a right-cloven family over their underlying sections (). In particular, an isomorphism of retractions of s from \mathopen {} \left ( r,h \right ) \mathclose {} to \mathopen {} \left ( r',h' \right ) \mathclose {} is given by a homotopy H_r \colon r \sim r' together with a higher homotopy H_h \colon h \sim \mathopen {} \left ( \lambda x \mathpunct {.} H_r \mathopen {} \left ( r x \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h'x \right ) \mathclose {}.

frontmatter.title: Path object structure on fibers

mainmatter.p: Given f \colon A \to B and b:B, we shall impose a path object structure on the fiber \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } fx=b of f over b. The family \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ( f-=b \right ) \mathclose {} \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} given by intrinsic path objects in each component is right-cloven over \mathord { \vartriangle } A:

ol.li: for p \colon x = y and \phi :fy=b, we define p^*b \colon fx=b to be identification \mathsf {act}_{ f } p \cdot \phi;
the unit law can be seen to hold using the unit laws for composition of identification.

mainmatter.p: Therefore, we may compute the contravariant sum \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } } { \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ( fx=b \right ) \mathclose {} } using the cleaving above, with \mathopen {} \left ( x, \phi \right ) \mathclose {} \approx _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } } { \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ( fx=b \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ( y, \psi \right ) \mathclose {} definitionally equal to \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p: x= y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p: x= y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p: x= y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p: x= y \right ) \mathclose {} } } \phi = \mathsf {act}_{ f } p \cdot \psi.

frontmatter.title: Dependent product of path objects

mainmatter.p: The product of a family \mathcal {B} \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} path objects is a path object.
Univalence follows from the closure of families of contractible types under dependent products () via the distributivity of \prod over \sum.

frontmatter.title: Restriction of path objects along quasi-inverses

mainmatter.p: Let \mathcal {A} be a path object and let B be a type. Given a quasi-inverse function g \colon B \to \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {}, we can restrict the path object \mathcal {A} to a path object structure g^* \mathcal {A} on B as follows:
mainmatter.tex: \begin {aligned} \mathopen {} \left \lvert g^* \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} &: \equiv B \\ x \approx _{ g^* \mathcal {A} } y &: \equiv gx \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } gy \\ \mathsf {rx}_{ g^* \mathcal {A} } x &: \equiv \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } \mathopen {} \left ( gx \right ) \mathclose {} \end {aligned}

mainmatter.p: Fixing x:B, we must show that \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:B \right ) \mathclose {} } } gx \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } gy is a proposition. But this is a retract of \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } gx \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y, which is a proposition by the univalence of \mathcal {A}; thus the result follows from the closure of propositions under retracts ().

frontmatter.title: The univalence principle for types

mainmatter.p: The reflexive graph structure on types given by equivalences is univalent. We shall write \mathsf {Type} to indicate the resulting (large) path object of types and equivalences.

frontmatter.title: Displayed path object

mainmatter.p: A displayed reflexive graph \mathcal {B} over a reflexive graph \mathcal {A} is called univalent when for each x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} the component \mathcal {B} \mathopen {} \left [ x \right ] \mathclose {} is univalent. We define a displayed path object to be a univalent displayed reflexive graph.
We will write \mathsf {DispPathObj} \, \mathcal {A} for the (large) type of displayed path objects over \mathcal {A}.

frontmatter.title: The intrinsic path object on a type

mainmatter.p: The intrinsic reflexive graph on a type A is a path object.
Univalence follows immediately by identification induction.

frontmatter.title: The intrinsic left cleaving of a family of path objects

mainmatter.p: Given a type A and a family of path objects \mathcal {B} \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj}, we can always define a left cleaving of \mathcal {B} over the intrinsic path object \mathord { \vartriangle } A. The left cleaving is obtained directly from the transport operation of the identification type.

frontmatter.title: The total path object of a displayed path object

mainmatter.p: Let \mathcal {A} be a path object, and let \mathcal {B} be a displayed path object over \mathcal {A}. Then the total reflexive graph \mathcal {A} . \mathcal {B} is a path object.
Univalence is established in two singleton inductions, first using univalence of \mathcal {A} followed by univalence of \mathcal {B}.

frontmatter.title: The univalence principle for dependent functions

mainmatter.p: The reflexive graph structure on \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx} by homotopy and identity homotopies is univalent.
